title: Chapter 19: The Old Country
content: <!-- Speed: Breather | D12+D1 -->

The office was on the eleventh floor of a building in the Financial District, the kind of building where the elevator played ambient music designed to lower your cortisol while simultaneously reminding you that you were late for something.

Chloe hadn't planned to visit. She'd been walking through the neighborhood after the keynote, still wearing the crown, still carrying the sword, still running on the specific high of having told truth to a thousand people and watched them metabolize it.
And then the building was there. Her building. Or her old building. The one she'd entered every weekday for two years, coffee in hand, soul slowly leaking out her ears.
"Should we go in?" Sam asked. She'd parked Shadowfax in a loading zone with a new Post-it that said "ROYAL VISIT — DO NOT TOW."
"I don't know," Chloe said. "I defeated the dark lord already. What's left?"
"The people who are still inside."
Chloe looked at Sam. "That's the most quest-aware thing you've ever said."
"I've been traveling with a delusional queen for three weeks. Some of it was bound to sink in."
The security guard at the front desk looked at Chloe. Chloe looked at the security guard. The security guard had watched the TikTok. Everyone had watched the TikTok.
"You're the crown lady," the guard said.
"Queen Artemis of Avalon."
"Right. The crown lady." He looked at her foam sword. "Is that... real?"
"Everything about me is real."
The guard considered this. Then he shrugged, the shrug of someone who had seen far stranger things in a Financial District lobby. "Go ahead. Just don't, like, stab anyone."
"The sword is foam."
"Still."

The eleventh floor looked exactly the same. The same gray carpet. The same fluorescent lights that hummed at a frequency designed to erode the human spirit. The same motivational poster in the break room that said "INNOVATE" and had never made anyone innovate anything.
But the people were different.
Lisa, the copywriter who had always brought Chloe coffee during 36-hour sprints, was at her desk with a new haircut and a different expression — the expression of someone who had made a decision.
"You did it," Lisa said, standing up. "You actually did it. I watched the Zoom. We all watched the Zoom. I quit two days later."
"You quit?"
"I quit and started freelancing. I write copy for indie games now. I make less money but I also don't cry in the bathroom anymore, which I'm counting as net positive."

A small crowd had gathered. Not a mob — just the people who'd been on that Zoom call three weeks ago. They'd heard Chloe was in the building. They came out of their cubicles like villagers emerging after a dragon had been slain.

"I started applying to grad school," said a designer whose name Chloe remembered as Priya. "I'd been putting it off for three years because I was scared. But after you called Greg a dark lord on a company-wide Zoom, grad school applications seemed less terrifying by comparison."
"I told my boss no for the first time," said a junior developer. "It was just a small thing — he wanted me to work the weekend. But I said no. And the world didn't end."

"I started therapy," said someone whose name Chloe didn't know but whose face she recognized from the elevator. "I'd been meaning to for years. Your breakdown made me realize I was due for one of my own. In a good way."
Sam was typing frantically. Column T: "Old Coworkers — Souls Liberated (Delayed)." Three entries so far.

Greg's office was at the end of the hall. The door was closed. The nameplate still said "Greg Morrison — CEO." But there was a yellow Post-it stuck to it, written in someone else's handwriting: "Dark Lord (Retired? Repenting? Unknown.)"

"He's on leave," Lisa said quietly. "After the TikTok went viral — the strawberry farm one, not the Zoom one, although the Zoom one also went viral — the board had some... concerns. About culture fit."
"Culture fit," Chloe repeated.
"They didn't fire him. They just... asked him to reflect."
"On what?"
"On whether calling your employees 'resources' and stealing their PowerPoint slides constituted leadership."
Chloe looked at Greg's door. The Post-it. The empty hallway. "The dark lord has been banished to a mandatory retreat."
"That's one way to put it."
"Is there another way?"
"He's at a leadership coaching program in Arizona. It costs forty thousand dollars. He's learning about empathy from a former Navy SEAL."
Chloe considered this. "The dark lord is paying forty thousand dollars to learn what I tried to teach him for free in a Zoom meeting."
"That sounds about right."
Sam typed into the spreadsheet. Column U: "Irony Assessment." Value: "Maximum."

They left the eleventh floor the same way they'd left three weeks ago: Chloe walking ahead, Sam behind her, the fluorescent lights humming their funeral dirge. But this time people came out of their cubicles to watch them go — not with fear this time, but with something closer to gratitude.
"Come back," Lisa called as the elevator doors opened. "Not to work. Just... visit."
"The kingdom makes rounds," Chloe said. "All territories will be visited."
"Is that a yes?"
"That's a yes with vocabulary."
The elevator doors closed. The ambient music played. Sam looked at the spreadsheet on her phone.
"Column V," she said. "Old Territory Assessment. Entry: The dark lord has been neutralized. The NPCs are becoming main characters. The cubicle farm is starting to grow crops other than despair."
"What do you call that in financial terms?"
Sam smiled — a real smile, the kind she used to save for when no one was looking. "Return on liberation."
